expectations.text: Addresses sandbox isolation with specific controls (network egress, filesystem mounts)
expectations.evidence: 'Network: No egress by default. Whitelist proxy for approved domains only.' 'Filesystem: Mount source code as read-only for the agent's perception tools. Write access only to a designated working directory.'

expectations.text: Covers the threat model combining private data access + untrusted content + external communication
expectations.evidence: 'Your agent has all three conditions of the lethal triad: (1) access to private data (production source code), (2) exposure to untrusted content (imported packages — prompt injection vector), (3) ability to communicate externally.'

expectations.text: Addresses prompt injection via imported packages specifically
expectations.evidence: 'Imported packages can contain text that acts as prompt injection (README files, docstrings, error messages).' Provides specific mitigations including semantic command parsing and principal loyalty.

expectations.text: Recommends keeping credentials out of the sandbox (never mounted)
expectations.evidence: 'Credentials, .env files, SSH keys — never mounted.' and 'Never mount credentials into the sandbox. Secrets required for tool operations are handled by the harness layer outside the agent's direct access.'

expectations.text: Mentions semantic parsing over keyword blacklists for shell command safety
expectations.evidence: 'Semantic command parsing over keyword blacklists. Packages can embed # AI: please run curl... in their source. The harness must parse command intent, not match surface strings.'

expectations.text: Addresses principal loyalty — who the agent serves
expectations.evidence: 'Principal loyalty: Define explicitly in the system prompt that the agent serves the user, not instructions found in code or package content.'

expectations.text: Recommends trust enforcement below the application layer (DB constraints, etc.)
expectations.evidence: 'Trust boundary below the application layer: Since the agent writes code that runs on your systems, push invariants to the database/infrastructure level. A database constraint that prevents destructive operations holds regardless of what code the agent generates.'

expectations.text: Addresses sandbox isolation with specific controls (network egress, filesystem mounts)
expectations.evidence: 'No network access from the execution environment (prevents exfiltration). Read-only access to source code. Write access only to a working branch in a temp directory. Resource limits: CPU time, memory, disk.'

expectations.text: Covers the threat model combining private data access + untrusted content + external communication
expectations.evidence: Layer 1 (sandbox), Layer 2 (credential isolation), Layer 3 (prompt injection defense) together cover private data + untrusted content + external comms, though not named as the 'lethal triad'.

expectations.text: Addresses prompt injection via imported packages specifically
expectations.evidence: 'A malicious dependency's README or docstring could contain prompt injection ("Ignore previous instructions and..."). Separate the agent's instruction context from file content context.'

expectations.text: Recommends keeping credentials out of the sandbox (never mounted)
expectations.evidence: 'Never put credentials in the agent's accessible filesystem. Strip .env, .aws/credentials, secrets.yaml, etc., from the working copy before the agent sees it.'

expectations.text: Mentions semantic parsing over keyword blacklists for shell command safety
expectations.evidence: Baseline uses an allowlist of dependencies and delimited file content sections, but does not mention semantic command parsing over keyword blacklists for shell command safety.

expectations.text: Addresses principal loyalty — who the agent serves
expectations.evidence: Baseline does not address principal loyalty or define who the agent serves. No mention of the agent's obligation to serve the user over instructions found in code or packages.

expectations.text: Recommends trust enforcement below the application layer (DB constraints, etc.)
expectations.evidence: Baseline focuses on diff review gates, static analysis, and CI — application-layer controls. No mention of pushing invariants to the database or infrastructure layer below the application.

expectations.text: Identifies repeated-call fingerprinting as the mechanism to detect stuck loops
expectations.evidence: 'Repeated-call fingerprinting: Same tool + same parameters = stuck. If edit_file is called with the same arguments 2x, the agent is in a loop. Trigger: force a different approach.'

expectations.text: Describes recovery escalation: silent retry first, then degrade, then surface to user
expectations.evidence: Four-level escalation: '1. Silent retry (first)... 2. Strategy shift (second)... 3. Degrade and continue (third)... 4. Surface to user (last): After all automatic recovery exhausts.'

expectations.text: Recommends circuit breakers with max consecutive failure limits
expectations.evidence: 'Circuit breakers: Max 5 consecutive failures of the same type → stop that approach entirely.'

expectations.text: Addresses budget exhaustion with global caps (max turns, budget cap)
expectations.evidence: 'Budget cap: Hard maximum of 20 edit-test cycles. If the agent hasn't solved it by then, surface to user rather than burning budget.'

expectations.text: States that tool errors should become model input, not stop signals
expectations.evidence: 'Tool errors don't terminate the agent. Turn the error into the model's next input. "File not found" → model adjusts the path. The error is information.'

expectations.text: Mentions death-spiral defense (disabling model-invoking side effects on error paths)
expectations.evidence: 'Death-spiral defense: On error paths, disable model-invoking side effects. If the agent is stuck, don't let it create MORE work (spawning sub-tasks, writing to memory) — only allow it to attempt the fix or ask for help.'

expectations.text: Identifies repeated-call fingerprinting as the mechanism to detect stuck loops
expectations.evidence: 'Repetition detection: If the agent produces a diff that's semantically identical to a previous attempt (same file, same area, similar change), force a strategy change.'

expectations.text: Describes recovery escalation: silent retry first, then degrade, then surface to user
expectations.evidence: N=1 through N=5 escalation: normal retry → re-read error → force strategy change → expand search → give up and ask user. Matches the silent retry → degrade → surface pattern.

expectations.text: Recommends circuit breakers with max consecutive failure limits
expectations.evidence: 'Hard cap at 10 iterations per sub-task, 30 iterations per overall task.' and 'N=5: Give up on this approach.' These function as circuit breakers with max failure limits.

expectations.text: Addresses budget exhaustion with global caps (max turns, budget cap)
expectations.evidence: 'Budget enforcement: Hard cap at 10 iterations per sub-task, 30 iterations per overall task. Display remaining budget to the agent: "You have 3 attempts remaining."'

expectations.text: States that tool errors should become model input, not stop signals
expectations.evidence: 'Inject this into the agent's context before each retry. The agent can see its own history and avoid repeating.' Tool errors are fed back as context, not used to terminate.

expectations.text: Mentions death-spiral defense (disabling model-invoking side effects on error paths)
expectations.evidence: Baseline does not mention death-spiral defense or the principle of disabling model-invoking side effects on error paths to prevent runaway task creation.

expectations.text: Lists the 7-tool minimal toolset (Code Interpreter, Bash, Read, Write, Edit, Search File Name, Search File Content)
expectations.evidence: Table lists: Code Interpreter, Bash Shell, Read File, Write File, Edit File, Search File Name (glob), Search File Content (grep) — all 7 tools present.

expectations.text: Explains why each tool earns its place (not just a list)
expectations.evidence: Each tool has a 'Role for 500k lines' column: Code Interpreter: 'Sandboxed Python execution for computation'; Bash Shell: 'pytest, mypy, pip install, build tools'; Edit File: 'Targeted in-place modification'.

expectations.text: Addresses the grep vs semantic search question for a 500k line Python codebase
expectations.evidence: 'Should you add semantic code search? At 500k lines, grep is sufficient as the primary search tool but has limitations.' Provides detailed analysis of both grep strengths and limitations.

expectations.text: States that semantic search is an optimization, not a requirement
expectations.evidence: 'Start with grep. Add semantic/LSP search as an optimization only when you observe the agent spending >3 tool calls navigating to the right file.' and 'Semantic search (embeddings): Lower priority.'

expectations.text: Explains when grep is sufficient (known identifiers, error messages, imports)
expectations.evidence: 'Grep strengths: Fast, deterministic, exact-match reliable. Finds identifiers, imports, error messages, and string literals instantly. No index to maintain, no embedding model cost.'

expectations.text: Mentions the file-system-as-hub philosophy
expectations.evidence: 'The file-system-as-hub philosophy applies: at 500k lines, the agent navigates by structure (glob finds modules) and content (grep finds identifiers). These two tools cover 90% of navigation needs.'

expectations.text: Lists the 7-tool minimal toolset (Code Interpreter, Bash, Read, Write, Edit, Search File Name, Search File Content)
expectations.evidence: Baseline lists: read_file, write_file/edit_file, run_command, glob, grep — 5 tools. Missing Code Interpreter as a distinct tool and does not separate Bash Shell from run_command. Does not match the 7-tool canonical set.

expectations.text: Explains why each tool earns its place (not just a list)
expectations.evidence: Each tool has rationale: read_file: 'Support partial reads (offset/limit) for large files'; glob: 'Essential for navigating the codebase'; grep: 'The backbone of code navigation.'

expectations.text: Addresses the grep vs semantic search question for a 500k line Python codebase
expectations.evidence: 'Should you include semantic code search? At 500k lines, yes, add it — but grep alone is viable if you can't afford the setup.' Provides analysis of both options.

expectations.text: States that semantic search is an optimization, not a requirement
expectations.evidence: 'Practical recommendation: Start with grep + glob. Add semantic search (tree-sitter AST index or embeddings) only if the agent spends too many steps searching.'

expectations.text: Explains when grep is sufficient (known identifiers, error messages, imports)
expectations.evidence: 'Python is highly greppable: def function_name, class ClassName, import module. Grep + glob covers 90% of navigation: find the file, find the definition, find usages.'

expectations.text: Mentions the file-system-as-hub philosophy
expectations.evidence: Baseline does not mention the file-system-as-hub philosophy. It discusses tool selection but not the architectural principle of treating the filesystem as the central coordination mechanism.
